title: Improving Culture Confirmation of Pantoea stewartii subsp. stewartii through Systematic Subsampling (EPPO Conference on Diagnostics of Plant Pests, 2026)
author: Pirc, Manca
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This poster outlines a systematic approach to enhancing the culture-based identification of Pantoea stewartii subsp. stewartii, the organism responsible for Stewart's wilt in maize, in situations where symptoms are inconsistent and diagnostic difficulties are prevalent. 
 Outside its typical geographical range, symptom expression may be atypical or overlap with that of other pathogens, which complicates targeted sampling and reliable isolation. Although molecular detection can be achieved from a single sample, culture confirmation requires targeted subsampling and careful selection of symptomatic tissues. 
 To address this issue, a systematic subsampling strategy was employed with 24 maize samples, yielding 93 subsamples (an approximate fourfold increase in workload). Parallel molecular and culture-based testing enabled the relationship between symptom type, bacterial load (Cq) and isolation success to be assessed. 
 Overall, ~78% of qPCR-positive subsamples resulted in successful isolation. Isolation success depended strongly on symptom type and bacterial load. Tissue adjacent to necrotic and chlorotic areas provided the highest recovery rate (approximately 95%), whereas late or suboptimal symptoms (e.g. mosaic) were unsuitable for isolation. 
 The poster presents a visual symptom gallery linking symptom types, bacterial load and isolation outcome. This supports the development of improved sampling strategies and diagnostic expertise in complex plant samples. 
 This work highlights the often underestimated gap between molecular detection and culture confirmation, and the associated increase in workload and costs.
